coachNote: Still the same session — I'm holding it one more day because your log for it hasn't reached me yet, and the SONGS block is the one I most want your honest read on before I build the next step. The headline hasn't changed: a right-hand melody floating over left-hand roots (in C on the I–V–vi–IV and on the 50s "Stand By Me" loop), plus the broken-chord roll grown to both hands and lifted into G — that's the real work of the day, so if you're short on time go straight to those four. Warm up with the recall scales first (C, A minor, A harmonic), then B♭ and E♭ from memory — say the fingering out loud before you play, since that IS your "memorize the fingerings" note turned into a drill. The G♭ payoff is waiting too: the full one-octave scale built around the thumb-tuck you already nailed, dead slow and even. When you finish, give me an honest 1–5 on each and tell me which of these melodies you recognize — or name a song you'd actually love to play — and that's what I'll shape the next session around.

exercises.label: C major — hands together, from memory
exercises.block: Recall warm-up · no fingerings on the staff
exercises.abc:
X:1
M:4/4
L:1/4
K:C
C D E F | G A B c |]
exercises.cue: Quick warm-up. Say "C major" out loud, then play it both hands from memory — no fingerings printed.
exercises.fingering: From memory — RH 1 2 3 1 2 3 4 5 · LH 5 4 3 2 1 3 2 1 (only if truly stuck)

exercises.label: A natural minor — hands together, from memory
exercises.block: Recall warm-up · no fingerings on the staff
exercises.abc:
X:1
M:4/4
L:1/4
K:Am
A, B, C D | E F G A |]
exercises.cue: Same white keys as C major — so how do you know it's A minor? It starts and rests on A. Name it, then play it.
exercises.fingering: From memory — RH 1 2 3 1 2 3 4 5 · LH 5 4 3 2 1 3 2 1 (only if stuck)

exercises.label: A harmonic minor — hands together, from memory
exercises.block: Recall warm-up · no fingerings on the staff
exercises.abc:
X:1
M:4/4
L:1/4
K:Am
A, B, C D | E F ^G A |]
exercises.cue: One note makes it "harmonic" instead of natural minor — the raised 7th, G♯. Name it, find it, play it from memory.
exercises.fingering: From memory — the only black key is the G♯ near the top

exercises.label: B♭ major — hands together, from memory
exercises.abc:
X:1
M:4/4
L:1/4
K:Bb
B, C D E | F G A B |]
exercises.cue: You owned this at a 5 — so now, your note: memorize the fingering. Page is blank. Picture where the 4th finger lands on the black key, then play it from memory. Peek only if you truly stall.
exercises.fingering: From memory — RH 2 1 2 3 1 2 3 4 · LH 3 2 1 4 3 2 1 2 (peek only if stuck)

exercises.label: E♭ major — hands together, from memory
exercises.abc:
X:1
M:4/4
L:1/4
K:Eb
E F G A | B c d e |]
exercises.cue: Climbed to a 5 last time — so it graduates to from-memory too. Three flats (B♭ E♭ A♭). Thumbs find the white keys, long fingers live on the black. Name your fingering before you start.
exercises.fingering: From memory — RH 2 1 2 3 4 1 2 3 · LH 3 2 1 4 3 2 1 2 (peek only if stuck)

exercises.label: A♭ major — right hand
exercises.cue: Holding at a 4 — keep it crisp and nudge the tempo. Thumb on C and F; finger 3 starts you. Fingerings stay printed here while it settles.

exercises.label: D♭ major — right hand
exercises.cue: Five flats, holding at 4 — a touch faster. Thumbs on the two white keys (F and C); everything else is black. Numbers stay on the page for now.

exercises.label: G♭ major — full scale, right hand (rebuilt from the tuck)
exercises.cue: Here's the payoff — you nailed the thumb-tuck drill (a 5!). Now we run the whole one-octave scale around it. The tuck is the same move you already own: after the 4th finger on B♭, thumb slides under to C♭, and again at the top. Slow and even, right hand only.
exercises.fingering: RH: 2 3 4 1 2 3 4 1 — the two thumb-tucks (at c and the top g) are the spots you just mastered

exercises.label: D natural minor — hands together
exercises.cue: Holding it steady — one flat (B♭), same finger pattern as C major in a darker key. Keep both hands locked together and even.

exercises.label: I–V–vi–IV in C — a song: melody over chords
exercises.block: Pop songs · melody in the right hand, chords in the left
exercises.abc:
X:1
M:4/4
L:1/4
K:C
V:1 clef=treble
V:2 clef=bass
[V:1] "C"E2 G2 | "G"D2 G2 | "Am"E2 A2 | "F"F2 A2 | "C"c2 G2 | "G"B2 G2 | "Am"A2 c2 | "F"c4 |]
[V:2] C,4 | G,4 | A,4 | F,4 | C,4 | G,4 | A,4 | F,4 |]
exercises.cue: The big step: your chords were too easy, so now you play a SONG. Same C–G–Am–F you own — but the left hand holds the root while the right hand sings a melody on top. This is what playing a song actually is. Left hand steady, right hand smooth and singing. Take it slow the first few passes.
exercises.fingering: LH: single root, finger 5, held each bar · RH: melody, mostly 1–3–5 shapes

exercises.label: Broken-chord pop pattern — I–V–vi–IV in C, hands together
exercises.block: Pop songs · melody in the right hand, chords in the left
exercises.abc:
X:1
M:4/4
L:1/4
K:C
V:1 clef=treble
V:2 clef=bass
[V:1] "C"C E G E | "G"G B d B | "Am"A c e c | "F"F A c A |]
[V:2] C,4 | G,4 | A,4 | F,4 |]
exercises.cue: The broken-chord roll you had at a 5 — now grown to both hands. Right hand rolls the chord (bottom–middle–top–middle), left hand anchors the root underneath. This is the engine of a hundred pop ballads. Keep the roll smooth and the left hand quiet and steady.
exercises.fingering: RH 1 3 5 3 on each chord · LH single root, finger 5

exercises.label: Broken-chord pop pattern — I–V–vi–IV in G, hands together
exercises.block: Pop songs · melody in the right hand, chords in the left
exercises.abc:
X:1
M:4/4
L:1/4
K:G
V:1 clef=treble
V:2 clef=bass
[V:1] "G"G B d B | "D"D F A F | "Em"E G B G | "C"C E G E |]
[V:2] G,4 | D,4 | E,4 | C,4 |]
exercises.cue: Same rolling pattern, lifted into G — a favorite pop and guitar key. The key signature hands you the F♯, so D major rolls as D–F♯–A. Meet D and Em as broken chords now, hands together. Smooth and even.
exercises.fingering: RH 1 3 5 3 on each chord · LH single root, finger 5

exercises.label: I–vi–IV–V in C — the "50s" loop as a song: melody over chords
exercises.block: Pop songs · melody in the right hand, chords in the left
exercises.abc:
X:1
M:4/4
L:1/4
K:C
V:1 clef=treble
V:2 clef=bass
[V:1] "C"G2 E2 | "Am"A2 c2 | "F"c2 A2 | "G"d2 B2 | "C"E2 G2 | "Am"c2 A2 | "F"A2 F2 | "G"B2 G2 |]
[V:2] C,4 | A,4 | F,4 | G,4 | C,4 | A,4 | F,4 | G,4 |]
exercises.cue: A second song shape — the "Stand By Me" loop (C–Am–F–G), now with a melody on top. Same idea: left hand holds the root, right hand sings. Notice how this loop feels different from the first one even with nearly the same chords. Slow and singing.
exercises.fingering: LH: single root, finger 5, held each bar · RH: melody, mostly 1–3–5 shapes
